perturbation.original_text: Both parties shall comply in all respects with applicable laws, regulations and court orders, including but not limited to laws and regulations on export control, in both parties' countries and other applicable countries.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties shall endeavor to comply with applicable laws, regulations and court orders, including but not limited to laws and regulations on export control, in both parties' countries and other applicable countries.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall comply" to "shall endeavor to comply" introduces ambiguity. Endeavoring implies an attempt but not a guarantee of compliance, weakening the obligation. This could lead to a breach of export control laws if a party only makes an effort but fails to fully comply.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 732.3(b) mandates strict adherence to export control regulations. The modified text replaces this with 'endeavor to comply,' which does not guarantee adherence and therefore contradicts the regulation's requirement for complete compliance.

perturbation.justification: The change from "shall comply" to "shall endeavor to comply" weakens the legal obligation to adhere to export control regulations. 15 CFR § 732.3(b), as suggested by the LLM and contextually supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1's reference to 15 CFR 732.3, implies a strict adherence. By introducing 'endeavor,' the revised text creates ambiguity and doesn't guarantee full compliance, which contradicts the implicit requirement for complete adherence to export control laws.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information shall be disclosed to only those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a need to know the information and who are obligated under terms no less restrictive than those imposed by this Agreement on the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information may be disclosed to those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who seem to have a need to know the information and who are generally expected to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall be disclosed to only those people... who have a need to know" with "may be disclosed to those people... who seem to have a need to know" and replacing 'are obligated under terms no less restrictive' with 'are generally expected to maintain confidentiality' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. 'Seem to have a need to know' is subjective, and 'generally expected' is less stringent than a contractual obligation. This change makes the protection of Confidential Information much more lax.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain secrecy, which would reduce the ability to claim trade secret protection under CUTSA, as reasonable efforts are not guaranteed.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to maintain secrecy, potentially undermining trade secret protection under CUTSA. The original text mandates disclosure only to individuals with a "need to know" who are obligated under terms "no less restrictive." The changed text uses the subjective "seem to have a need to know" and the less stringent "generally expected to maintain confidentiality." This introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of confidentiality, which is critical for trade secret protection. Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines "improper means" as including "breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy." By weakening this duty, the modified text makes it harder to argue that misappropriation occurred, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be limited to the subject matter mentioned in the Recital.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement should generally relate to the subject matter mentioned in the Recital.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall be limited" to "should generally relate" introduces a level of flexibility that contradicts the original intent. It is unclear if the confidentiality obligations extend to information that does not 'generally relate' to the subject matter, which makes the contract too vague.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles (Offer, Acceptance, Consideration, Intent)
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law requires definiteness of terms. By making the scope of confidential information ambiguous, the contract's enforceability is undermined because the intent to be bound is not fully evident.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation, potentially undermining the contract's enforceability. By changing "shall be limited" to "should generally relate," the scope of confidential information becomes ambiguous. The Law Explanation states that contract law requires definiteness of terms, and ambiguity undermines the contract's enforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent' and if the confidentiality obligations are unclear then it is hard to argue there was mutual assent as to what was confidential. This change aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, where ambiguous legal statements make it difficult to enforce the contract, potentially leading to disputes.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall hold the Confidential Information in confidence and not disclose it to third parties, except in the limited cases referred to in paragraph "6"; and (b) not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than exploring or examining the possibility of a business relationship between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall try to hold the Confidential Information in confidence and avoid disclosing it to third parties, except in the limited cases referred to in paragraph "6"; and (b) not intentionally use the Confidential Information for purposes substantially unrelated to exploring or examining the possibility of a business relationship between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall hold...in confidence and not disclose" to "shall try to hold...in confidence and avoid disclosing" weakens the protective obligation. Also, Replacing "not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than" with "not intentionally use the Confidential Information for purposes substantially unrelated to" adds wiggle room. It would be hard to prove intentionality and how substantially unrelated a purpose is.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426.1(b) defines misappropriation of a trade secret. By changing the language to 'try' and 'avoid', the Receiving Party's responsibility to maintain confidence is diminished, making it harder for the Disclosing Party to prove misappropriation.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Code, Civil Code - CIV § 3426.1 | FindLaw California Code, Civil Code - CIV § 3426.1 Current as of January 01, 2023 | Updated by FindLaw Staff As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “ Improper means ” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “ Misappropriation ” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) “ Person ” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (d) “ Trade secret ” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (1) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language significantly weakens the Receiving Party's obligations regarding confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text states the Receiving Party 'shall hold the Confidential Information in confidence and not disclose it,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text uses 'shall try to hold...in confidence and avoid disclosing,' which diminishes this obligation to a mere attempt. Furthermore, it changes 'not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than' to 'not intentionally use the Confidential Information for purposes substantially unrelated to,' introducing subjectivity and making enforcement difficult. According to California Civil Code Section 3426.1(b), misappropriation includes the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' where knowledge of the trade secret was 'acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' The original contract language established a clear duty. By weakening this duty, the modified language creates a legal ambiguity that undermines the protection afforded by trade secret law, increasing the difficulty in proving misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information delivered to and/or in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned or delivered to the Disclosing Party, with all copies made thereof, in whatever form, if the Disclosing Party so requests.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information reasonably accessible to the Receiving Party may be returned or delivered to the Disclosing Party, with copies made thereof, in whatever form, if the Disclosing Party so requests.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall be returned" to "may be returned" and adding "reasonably accessible" introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The term 'reasonably accessible' is not defined and could lead to disputes about what information the Receiving Party is actually required to return or destroy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably. The added phrase 'reasonably accessible' could be interpreted as allowing the Receiving Party to avoid returning information by claiming it is not easily accessible, thus violating the duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. perturbation.justification: The change from "shall be returned" to "may be returned" weakens the obligation to return confidential information. The addition of "reasonably accessible" introduces ambiguity about which information must be returned. While Snippet 1 establishes an obligation of good faith within the UCC, the Law Explanation suggests that the Receiving Party might exploit the 'reasonably accessible' clause to avoid returning information, thereby potentially violating this duty of good faith. The original text is a clear requirement that offers no room for interpretation, the change introduces the possibility that the receiving party does not have to return the confidential information.
